title: Cat Black Grit in Ears and Head Shaking: Causes and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticing black grit in your cat's ears? Learn about ear mites, yeast infections, and why this common issue is usually a low-urgency vet visit.
content_html:
What is the Black Grit in Your Cat’s Ears?

Finding dark, debris-like material that resembles coffee grounds inside your cat's ears is a common sign of an external ear issue. Most frequently, this black grit is a combination of earwax, blood, and waste produced by ear mites (Otodectes cynotis). However, similar symptoms can also be caused by a yeast or bacterial infection. The head shaking you are seeing is your cat's way of trying to relieve the intense itching and irritation caused by these triggers.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. While seeing your cat in discomfort is distressing, black ear grit and head shaking are rarely life-threatening. You should schedule an appointment with your veterinarian within the next 24 to 48 hours. This is not a condition that will resolve on its own, and delayed treatment can lead to secondary infections or damage to the ear canal from excessive scratching.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, close-up photo of the inside of the ear flap and the debris itself can be very helpful for your veterinary team. A high-quality image allows a professional to assess the color, texture, and volume of the discharge. This helps them determine whether the issue is likely parasitic or inflammatory, allowing them to prioritize your appointment and provide specific advice on whether you should avoid cleaning the ears before your visit.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter ear mite treatments?
faqs.answer: It is safer to wait for a veterinary diagnosis. If your cat has a ruptured eardrum from scratching, some over-the-counter medications can cause permanent deafness or neurological issues.

faqs.question: Are ear mites contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, ear mites are highly contagious. If you have other cats or dogs in the home, they may also need to be checked or treated to prevent the infection from passing back and forth.

faqs.question: How do I know if it is an infection or mites?
faqs.answer: It is difficult to tell with the naked eye. A veterinarian will use an otoscope to look deep into the canal and perform a 'swab' to look for mites or yeast under a microscope.
